era_context: The late 15th century continues as a period of European maritime expansion, Ottoman consolidation, and Ming stability. This year falls during the early phase of the Age of Exploration, with Portuguese and Spanish expeditions actively seeking new trade routes and territories. The Italian Wars are ongoing, and the Iberian kingdoms are consolidating power following the completion of the Reconquista.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asian kingdoms beyond Portuguese contact
Pacific Islands and Polynesia (no written records)
North American interior (no European contact yet; indigenous records non-existent in written form)

events.title: Vasco da Gama's fleet departs Calicut, beginning return voyage to Portugal
events.description: After establishing initial contact with the Zamorin of Calicut and acquiring spices and other goods, Vasco da Gama's fleet departs India in August 1498 to return to Portugal. The return voyage is arduous, with the fleet facing monsoon winds, disease, and navigation challenges. The voyage takes nearly a year, with the fleet rounding the Cape of Good Hope and arriving in Lisbon in September 1499. This return voyage confirms the viability of the sea route to India and establishes the foundation for sustained Portuguese maritime commerce in the Indian Ocean.

sources.name: Roteiro da Viagem de Vasco da Gama (Roteiro), contemporary account
Portuguese maritime records and expedition logs

events.certainty_note: The departure date from Calicut in August 1498 is well-attested in contemporary Portuguese records and the Roteiro.

events.title: Savonarola executed in Florence
events.description: Girolamo Savonarola, the Dominican friar and religious reformer who had dominated Florentine politics and culture for several years, is executed by hanging and burning on 23 May 1498. Savonarola had preached against corruption in the Church and secular society, leading a theocratic government in Florence from 1494 to 1498. His execution marks the end of his influence and the restoration of secular Medici control over Florence. The execution reflects the tension between religious reform movements and established political and ecclesiastical authorities in late 15th-century Italy.
key_figures: Pope Alexander VI (Borgia pope, opponent of Savonarola)

sources.name: Accounts by Savonarola's followers and opponents

events.certainty_note: The execution date of 23 May 1498 is well-documented in Florentine and papal records. Savonarola's life and death are extensively chronicled by contemporary observers.

events.title: Christopher Columbus undertakes his third voyage to the Americas
events.description: Christopher Columbus departs Spain in May 1498 on his third voyage to the Americas, commanding a fleet of six ships. The voyage explores the coast of South America, including Trinidad and the Orinoco River delta, and establishes the settlement of Santo Domingo on Hispaniola. Columbus's observations of the Orinoco River's massive freshwater discharge lead him to theorize that he has discovered a new continent, not merely islands off Asia. However, Columbus's governance of the colonies proves problematic, leading to conflicts with settlers and the Spanish crown. The voyage marks a turning point in Columbus's fortunes, as his administrative failures begin to undermine his authority.
key_figures: Christopher Columbus (Genoese explorer in Spanish service)
King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella of Spain (patrons)
Francisco de Bobadilla (Spanish official, later investigator of Columbus)
Indigenous peoples of the Caribbean and South American coast

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by crew members and Spanish officials

events.certainty_note: The departure date (May 1498) and general route of Columbus's third voyage are well-documented in Spanish records and Columbus's own writings.

events.title: Lodovico Sforza loses control of Milan to French forces
events.description: Lodovico Sforza, Duke of Milan, loses control of his duchy to French forces under King Louis XII in September 1498. Sforza had invited French intervention against rival powers but is unable to maintain control as French armies occupy Milan. Sforza flees to the Tyrol and later to the Holy Roman Empire. The loss of Milan marks a major shift in Italian politics, establishing French dominance in northern Italy and initiating a period of foreign intervention in Italian affairs that will characterize the Italian Wars for decades. The event demonstrates the vulnerability of Italian city-states to larger European powers.
key_figures: Maximilian I, Holy Roman Emperor (Sforza's ally)

sources.name: French royal records and military accounts
Contemporary Italian chronicles and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The fall of Milan to French forces in September 1498 is well-documented in both French and Italian sources. The date and circumstances are extensively chronicled.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet undertakes sixth voyage from China
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Arabian Peninsula
events.description: The sixth voyage of Zheng He's treasure fleet departs from China, continuing the pattern of massive maritime expeditions that have characterized the early Ming period. The fleet explores the Indian Ocean, visiting East African ports, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Persian Gulf. The voyage demonstrates continued Chinese maritime capability and diplomatic reach, though the expeditions do not result in permanent territorial expansion or the establishment of trading monopolies. The voyages are primarily diplomatic and trade missions, aimed at demonstrating Ming power and collecting tribute from foreign rulers.

sources.name: Ming court records and imperial archives (Mingshi)
The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (Yingyai Shenglan) by Ma Huan
Historiographic consensus from Chinese maritime history

events.certainty_note: The exact dating of Zheng He's voyages remains debated among scholars. The 1498 date for the sixth voyage is approximate, as sources do not provide precise departure and return dates for individual voyages. Some scholars argue for different chronologies.

events.title: Vasco da Gama reaches Malindi, East Africa, on return voyage
events.description: During his return voyage from India, Vasco da Gama's fleet reaches Malindi on the East African coast in early 1499 (some sources date this to late 1498). The fleet takes on supplies and repairs before continuing the journey around the Cape of Good Hope to Portugal. The stop at Malindi demonstrates the importance of East African ports in the Indian Ocean trade network and establishes initial Portuguese contact with East African rulers. The voyage's success in reaching India and returning to Europe establishes the viability of the sea route and sets the stage for sustained Portuguese maritime expansion in the Indian Ocean.

sources.name: Roteiro da Viagem de Vasco da Gama (Roteiro), contemporary account

events.certainty_note: The stop at Malindi is attested in the Roteiro and Portuguese records, though the exact date is uncertain—sources place it in late 1498 or early 1499. The 1498 dating is approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of Zheng He's voyages remains contested among scholars. Some historians argue that the traditional 'seven voyages' framework is a later historiographic construction and that the actual number and dates of voyages differ significantly from conventional accounts. Additionally, the significance attributed to Columbus's third voyage is complicated by the fact that his governance failures and the subsequent investigation by Bobadilla (1500) suggest that Columbus's achievements were already being questioned by Spanish authorities within a few years of the voyage. The narrative of Columbus as a triumphant discoverer is undermined by contemporary accounts of his administrative incompetence and harsh treatment of settlers.
historiographic_note: The year 1498 is well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding maritime exploration and Italian politics. However, this creates a significant Eurocentric bias: European activities are extensively chronicled while contemporary developments in Asia, Africa, and the Americas outside European contact zones are underrepresented. Ottoman and Ming sources exist but are less accessible to Western historians. Indigenous American perspectives are entirely absent from written records. The historical record is shaped by the interests of literate elites and merchants, with little representation of common people or non-literate societies. Portuguese and Spanish sources dominate the exploration narrative, while the perspectives of Indian, East African, and other non-European peoples are largely absent or filtered through European accounts.

graph_edges.note: Da Gama's departure from Calicut in 1498 led to his arrival in Portugal in 1499, confirming the viability of the sea route
Savonarola's execution in 1498 resulted from his rise to power in 1494 and subsequent conflicts with the papacy and secular authorities
The loss of Milan in 1498 is part of the broader Italian Wars that began in 1494
Columbus's third voyage and administrative failures led to his arrest by Bobadilla in 1500
